coredata.dc:title: Improved corrosion resistant performance of mild steel under acid environment by novel carbon dots as green corrosion inhibitor
coredata.prism:publicationName: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects
coredata.dc:creator: Saraswat, Vandana
Yadav, Mahendra
coredata.dc:description: A novel eco-friendly, water soluble and effective corrosion inhibitor N, S-CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The characterization of N, S-CDs was carried out by Fourier transformation infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photo electron spectroscopy (XPS) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The inhibitive effect of N, S-CDs on mild steel (MS) corrosion was evaluated by weight loss analysis, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization methods in 15% HCl solution. As per potentiodynamic polarization studies, studied inhibitor acts as mixed type inhibitor. Investigated N, S-CDs exhibited 98.64% of inhibition efficiency at 200 mg / L concentration and 303 K temperature. Investigated N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitor shows better corrosion inhibition efficiency as compared to the most of the similar type of corrosion inhibitors already reported in literature. The adsorption of N, S-CDs on MS surface followed Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The surface morphology of MS specimens was studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and contact angle measurement.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Corrosion inhibitor
Mild steel
Acid environment

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Colloids and Surfaces A: Physicochemical and Engineering Aspects

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Effect of exposure time on corrosion inhibition performance of inhibitors

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: N, S-CDs inhibitor characterization (a) HRTEM image (b) FTIR spectra (c) Raman spectra (d) UV-Vis absorption spectra, insert image of the N, S-CDs under daylight (left) and UV light (right) (e) Photoluminescence spectra.
XPS spectra of synthesized N, S-CDs inhibitor.
XPS spectra of synthesized N, S-CDs inhibitor for elemental analysis.
High resolution deconvulated spectra of (a) C1s (b) N1s (c) O1s (d) S2p.
Variation of C R and η % with inhibitor concentration at different temperature for N, S-CDs inhibitor.
Effect of exposure period on corrosion inhibitive properties of the studied inhibitors; (a) variation of corrosion rate with time (b) variation of corrosion inhibition efficiency with time.
Arrhenius (a) and Transition (b) plots for N, S-CDs inhibitor.
Langmuir plots for N, S-CDs inhibitor at (a) 303 K (b) 313 K (c) 323 K (d) 333 K.
Potentiodynamic polarization curves of N, S-CDs inhibitor.
Nyquist plot (a) and equivalent circuit (b) of N, S-CDs.
Bode plots of N, S-CDs.
Contact angle measurement on MS surface (a) planer (b) blank (c) with N, S-CDs.
SEM images of MS (a) polished (b) blank (c) with N, S-CDs.
AFM images of MS (a) polished sample (b) blank sample (c) sample with N, S-CDs (d) height profile of polished sample (e) height profile of blank sample (f) height profile of sample with N, S-CDs.
Complex spectra of inhibited sample.
High resolution deconvulated spectra of (a) C1s (b) N1s (c) O1s (d) S2p (e) Fe 2p.
Synthetic routes for N, S-CDs inhibitor.
Corrosion parameters for N, S-CDs inhibitor.
The corrosion rate and percentage inhibition with and without 200 mg/ L of N, S-CDs inhibitor in 15% HCl at several exposure periods.
Kinetic parameters for N, S-CDs inhibitor.
Adsorption parameters of N, S-CDs inhibitor.
Potentiodynamic polarization parameters of N, S-CDs inhibitor for MS in 15% HCl solution.
EIS parameters of N, S-CDs inhibitor for MS in 15% HCl solution.

head.ce:title: Improved corrosion resistant performance of mild steel under acid environment by novel carbon dots as green corrosion inhibitor

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, IIT (ISM), Dhanbad, Jharkhand 826004, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, IIT (ISM) Dhanbad, Jharkhand –826004, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: A novel eco-friendly, water soluble and effective corrosion inhibitor N, S-CDs was synthesized by hydrothermal method. The characterization of N, S-CDs was carried out by Fourier transformation infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), ultraviolet-visible spectroscopy (UV–vis) spectroscopy, photoluminescence (PL), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photo electron spectroscopy (XPS) and high resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM). The inhibitive effect of N, S-CDs on mild steel (MS) corrosion was evaluated by weight loss analysis, electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) and potentiodynamic polarization methods in 15% HCl solution. As per potentiodynamic polarization studies, studied inhibitor acts as mixed type inhibitor. Investigated N, S-CDs exhibited 98.64% of inhibition efficiency at 200 mg / L concentration and 303 K temperature. Investigated N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitor shows better corrosion inhibition efficiency as compared to the most of the similar type of corrosion inhibitors already reported in literature. The adsorption of N, S-CDs on MS surface followed Langmuir adsorption isotherm. The surface morphology of MS specimens was studied by scanning electron microscopy (SEM), atomic force microscopy (AFM) and contact angle measurement.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The raw/processed data required to reproduce these findings cannot be shared at this time due to technical or time limitations.

ce:section.ce:para: Corrosion is a serious problem faced by numerous industries during various applications such as chemical cleaning, water treatment, acid pickling and acidization of oil well which leads to environmental problem and economic losses [1]. MS is one of the most important structural material used in pipelines (oil and gas transportation), infrastructures (building and bridges) and downhole casing due to its low cost, significant mechanical characteristics and ease of availability [2,3]. Most of the industrial processes such as acid descaling and oil well acidization are carried out in acidic environment [4] because they remove deposited salts, scales and increases the production of oil. But the problem with MS is that it is highly prone to corrosion in acidic medium [5]. Therefore, it is necessary to make effective strategies for corrosion mitigation of MS in these processes. One of the anticorrosive methods to protect and mitigate undesirable corrosion during these processes is the utilization of corrosion inhibitors as they adsorb on the surface of metal by making protective film. The compounds composed with N, S, O and π electrons in their structure are reported as acid inhibitors [6]. These types of molecules adsorbed on metal surface and reduce the area of contact between aggressive solution and metal surface by making a shield on surface of metal [7,8]. Currently, the inhibition effect of numerous organic and inorganic compounds has been reported [9–16] but many of them are highly toxic and have adverse impact on environment. The toxic nature and high cost of inorganic and organic inhibitors forced the corrosion scientist to find eco-friendly, cost effective and easily available corrosion inhibitors. The restriction in the application of inorganic corrosion inhibitors such as dichromats, chromates, arsenates and phosphates are due to their toxic nature [16]. The presence of heavy metals in inorganic inhibitors are detrimental for animals, human health, plants and have bad effect on ecology of environment, ground water quality and surface. The utilization of chromium salts as corrosion inhibitors has been limited because it is highly toxic and carcinogenic. Chrome phosphate, strontium chromate and zinc chromate etc. used as corrosion inhibitor are also carcinogenic. Generally, mixture of HF-HNO3 is used for the stainless-steel chemical pickling which produces nitrous gas and nitrous effluents, which creates pollution to the environment. The increase in awareness towards ecology and environmental pollution have forced researchers to make eco-friendly, water soluble and cost-effective corrosion inhibitor [17].

Carbon dots (CDs) are non-dimensional and fluorescent nanomaterials. Presently, carbon dots become research hotspot due to their stable fluorescence and chemical properties, biocompatibility and low in toxicity [18]. Because of all these outstanding properties, CDs have applicable in photocatalysis, bio-sensing, drug carriers, bio-imaging and in other fields of biology [19]. So far, many researchers prepared carbon dots by hydrothermal, laser ablation and microwave radiation [20,21]. Doping approach can effectively enhances CDs features such as spectrum range, fluorescence yield and many other properties, particularly by heteroatom doping of CDs [22]. Many researchers utilized CDs as good corrosion inhibitor for different metals in different environments. Wang et. al. reported N-CDs as the corrosion inhibitor by using 4-aminosalicylic acid as starting material against Q235 carbon steel in HCl medium which shows corrosion efficiency of 87.2% at 100 mg/L of concentration [23]. Li et. al. prepared N-CQDs by microwave treatment of EDTA, sulfamic acid and urotropine as raw materials and applied as corrosion inhibitor for N80 steel in 3% NaCl solution and got 83.7% efficiency at 600 mg/L of concentration [24]. Ye et. al. synthesized functionalized carbon dots by taking L-histidine and citric acid as precursor and found 90% efficiency at 100 mg/L of concentration for Q235 steel in 0.1 M HCl solution[25]. Cui et al. synthesized N-doped CDs from methacrylic acid and ethyl(methyl)amine and examined its inhibitive nature on Q235 steel in HCl and NaCl solution, got 93.93% and 88.96% efficiency at 200 mg / L of concentration [26]. Wang et. al. prepared N-CDs by facile hydrothermal method by using aminosalicylic acid as raw material and got 89.2% inhibition efficiency at 50 mg/L of concentration against copper in HCl solution [27]. Zhao. et. al synthesized carbon dots from imidazole ionic liquids and citric acid and used it as corrosion inhibitor against Q235 steel in 1 M HCl and 3.5 wt% NaCl solution and found 92.6% and 83.45% efficiency, respectively at 200 mg/L of concentration [28]. Cui et. al. synthesized N, S-CDs by using citric acid and thiourea as precursor for corrosion inhibition of Q235 carbon steel in 1 M HCl solution and got 96.6% inhibition efficiency at 400 mg/L of concentration [29]. Wang et al. prepared N-CDs through pyrolysis of ammonium citrate for 1, 2 and 3 h and evaluated their corrosion inhibition efficiency on Q235 steel in 1 M HCl and found 74%, 77% and 84% efficiency at 200 mg/L of concentration [30]. Cui et al. synthesized N-doped CDs from p-phenylenediamine and o-phenylenediamine and examined its inhibitive nature on carbon steel in HCl, got 83.1% and 81.9% efficiency at 200 mg/L of concentration [31]. Cen et al. prepared CDs by aminosalicylic acid and thiourea as initial material and reported its corrosion inhibition efficiency on carbon steel in NaCl solution as 93% at 50 mg / L of concentration [32].

Keeping in view the good corrosion inhibition efficiency of carbon dots we planned to dope the carbon dots with the elements having electron donating capability such a N and S atoms with an expectation to increase the inhibition efficiency of carbon dots. In present case, N, S-CDs were synthesized using citric acid, isoniazid and thiourea through hydrothermal method and applied as corrosion inhibitor on MS in 15% HCl solution. The characterization of N, S-CDs were carried out by FTIR, UV–vis, PL, Raman spectroscopy, XPS and HRTEM. Weight loss analysis, potentiodynamic polarization and EIS was performed to evaluate the inhibition efficiency of studied carbon dot. The surface morphology of MS specimens was studied by using SEM, AFM, XPS and contact angle measurement.

The synthesis of N, S-CDs was carried out by hydrothermal method. Mixture of citric acid (0.38 g, 2 mmol), isoniazid (0.411 g, 3 mmol) and thiourea (0.76 g, 10 mmol) were dissolved in 20 mL of water with ultrasonication. Then, dissolved mixture was transferred to teflon autoclave and kept in oven for 6 h at 180° C temperatures. Transparent product was achieved after cooling at room temperature. Then, the mixture was filtered by filter paper and got the desirable product. Scheme 1.

HRTEM images of studied carbon dots were taken by Talos F200X G2. XPS analysis was done by the 5000 versa probe ׀׀׀. FTIR spectra was captured by Agilent Cary 660. UV–vis spectroscopy and PL spectra of N, S-CDs was taken by UV- Agilent technologies Cary 8454 UV–vis and FI-Hitachi F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer, respectively.

In present study, MS sample with composition: C, 0.31; Si, 0.19; S, 0.008; Mn, 0.92; P, 0.010; and Fe balance were used. The size of the sample for both weight loss and electrochemical analysis were 6.0 × 2.5 × 0.1 cm and 1.0 × 1.0 × 0.1 cm respectively. Before both analysis (weight loss and electrochemical) MS samples were polished with the numerous grade (50, 80, 120, 220, 320, 400, 600, 800, 1000, 1500 and 2000) of abrasive paper, good polishing of each sample before carried out the experiment is necessary because it increases the smoothness, appearance which leads to the good results of weight loss and electrochemical. After polishing MS samples were cleaned by acetone and dried before use.

The test solution (15% HCl) was prepared with analytical grade 37% HCl by diluting it with doubly distilled water. The test solution volume taken for weight loss and electrochemical analysis was 150 mL. This testing solution was utilized in making different concentration of studied inhibitor.

The freshly prepared MS samples were weighed and immersed in 250 mL of beakers carrying 150 mL of 15% HCl solutions sustained 303–333 K of temperature with the help of thermostat water bath. After accomplishment of immersion of sample for 6 h, samples were taken out and corrosion product was removed. The samples were weighed again and change in weight was recorded. Weight loss analysis was carried out triplicate and average rate of corrosion were calculated. Weight loss analysis was performed for time of 6 h, according to ASTM [33]. The rate of corrosion (C R), inhibition efficiency (ƞ%)and surface coverage (θ) were calculated by the following equation:
(1)
C
R
=
87.6
W
ATD
(2)
ƞ % =
C
R
0
−
C
R
i
C
R
0
× 100
(3)
θ =
C
R
0
−
C
R
i
C
R
0

The electrochemical analysis was performed on CHI760D by using three-electrode assembly involving MS as working electrode with 1 cm2 uncovered area, saturated calomel electrode (SCE) as reference electrode and platinum (Pt) as counter electrode. Before doing the measurement, open circuit potential (OCP) was established by keeping three-electrode assembly without disturbance for 1800 s for each measurement. Each measurement was accomplished at 303 K. The polarization measurement was accomplished by sweeping the potential range of −200 to + 200 mV vs SCE with 1 mV s−1 scan rate. The anodic and cathodic curves of linear Tafel segment were extrapolated to acquire corrosion current density (i corr) and corrosion potential (E Corr). The ƞ% was evaluated by following equation.
(4)
ƞ % =
i
corr
−
i
corr
0
i
corr
The 5 mV amplitude and frequency range of 100 kHz to 10 mHz, was taken for EIS measurement.

SEM micrographs of specimens were captured by HITACHI S3400N. For this analysis, MS sample of dimension 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 0.1 cm were scraped away with several grade of emery papers, washed with doubly distilled water and acetone. The dried sampled were submerged in 15% HCl solution with and without N, S-CDs at 200 mg/ L of dosage at 303 K for 6 h, after completion of 6 h, samples were taken out, washed with doubly distilled water, acetone and dried then SEM micrographs were recorded.

AFM images were taken by the Agilent technology 5500 series. For AFM analysis, MS samples of dimension 1.0 cm × 1.0 cm × 0.1 cm were abraded with several grade of emery papers washed with distilled water and dried. The dried sampled were submerged in 15% HCl solution with and without N, S-CDs at 200 mg/ L of dosage at 303 K for 6 h, after completion of 6 h, samples were taken out, washed with doubly distilled water, acetone and dried then AFM micrographs were recorded. Contact angle measurement was carried out by KRUSSDSA25 instrument.

The synthesis of N, S-CDs was accomplished by heating isoniazid, thiourea and citric acid in water for 6 h at 180 °C. HRTEM analysis was performed to know the morphology and structure of carbon dots. Fig. 1(a) displayed the HRTEM image of synthesized N, S-CDs. It is clear from Fig. 1(a) that synthesized N, S-CDs are unevenly spherical shaped and well separated from each other, which revealed that no aggregation of particles occurs. The average particle size was found as 1.23 nm.

To get the detailed information about the chemical nature of prepared carbon dots and their surface chemistry, FTIR spectra of N, S-CDs was performed as displayed in Fig. 1(b). The N, S-CDs exhibited broad band at 3440 cm−1 assigned as combined peak of O–H and N–H [24]. The absorption peak at 2065 cm−1 was due to cumulative double bonds absorption present in NCS and NCO [24]. The absorption peak at 1630 and 1505 cm−1was assigned for CO [34] and CN [35], respectively. The peak at 1390 cm−1 is assigned for C–N [36] bond formation. Moreover, the absorption peak at 1090 cm−1 suggested the presence of CS [37] and absorption band at 625 cm−1 indicates the existence of C–S [37].

Raman spectroscopy is most powerful technique to characterize carbon-based nanomaterials. It is performed to identify different state of carbon. The Raman spectrum of prepared S, N-CDs is displayed in Fig. 1(c). The Fig. 1(c) represents two broad peaks at around 1337 and 1584 cm−1 related to the D and G bands, respectively. The D-band is ascribed to the sp3 hybridized carbon and G-band is attributed to the sp2 hybridized carbon. The relative D and G-band intensity ratio (ID/IG) for investigated N, S-CDs was found as 0.95, suggesting the graphitization degree [24].

The optical properties of synthesized carbon dots were studied by UV-Vis and photoluminescence. The UV absorption spectrum of N, S-CDs as displayed in Fig. 1(d) possess two evident absorption bands. The first absorption band at 273 nm was ascribed to the π-π*transition of sp2domain [32] and the second band at 330 nm was associated to the n-π*transition of CN bond [32]. The trapping of excited energy from the passivated surface heteroatom’s resulted in the absorption, which may contribute to the strong fluorescence. The N, S-CDs displayed green fluorescence as shown in Fig. 1(e). The fluorescence spectrum shown in Fig. 1(e) representing the maximum emission at 444 nm.

In order to identify the elements present in the synthesized N, S-CDs and their bonding, XPS was performed as shown in Fig. 2. It is evident from Fig. 2 that investigated N, S CDs consist of C, N, S and O elements. The atomic percentage of C, N, S and O elements in the synthesized N, S CDs was also determined by XPS as shown in Fig. 3. The atomic percentage of C, N, S and O elements in the synthesized N, S CDs was found as 69.8%, 13.8%, 1.9% and 14.5%, respectively.

The high resolution deconvulated XPS spectra of C1s, for N, S CDs is displayed in Fig. 4(a). Five peaks are detected at 284.0 (CC/C-C) [38], 284.6 (C–N) [39], 285.0 (CN) [40], 286.2 (CO/ CS)[18,41] and 287.9 (C–S)[24]. Fig. 4(b) displayed N1s spectra, where three peaks at 398.5 (CN) [37], 399.6 (C-N) [37] and 400.3 (N–H) [42] are observed. The O 1s spectra as displayed in Fig. 4(c) could be divided into two peaks at 531.19 and 532.3 eV corresponding to the CO and C–OH, respectively [6]. The S 2p spectrum as Fig. 4(d), could be divided into three peaks at 161, 162.2 and 163.5 eV allotted to the atomic sulfur, S 2p 3/2 and S 2p 1 /2, respectively. The S 2p 3/2 and S 2p 1 /2 binding energy are assigned to the formation of CS and C-S bonds [43].

Both nitrogen and sulfur XPS spectra confirms the successful doping of nitrogen and sulfur. XPS analysis results were accordance with the FTIR spectra results and further confirm the formation of desirable product.

The corrosion parameters (C R, ƞ%, θ) after 6 h of immersion with and without inhibitor at different temperatures (303–333 K) were calculated by using weight loss analysis and obtained corrosion parameters are depicted in Table 1. The variation of corrosion rate and inhibition efficiency with concentration of N, S-CDs at different temperatures is shown in Fig. 5. Table 1, revealed that the inhibition efficiency increases whereas corrosion rate decreases with increase in concentration of N, S-CDs. This finding can be ascribed as on increasing the concentration of inhibitor, the number of molecules of studied inhibitor adsorbed on the MS surface increases, which leads to the decrease in rate of metal dissolution process. The ƞ% of S, N-CDs enhanced up to 200 mg/L of concentration and remains unchanged after 200 mg/L concentration. Hence 200 mg/L was taken as optimum concentration for further study. The ƞ% of S, N-CDs at 200 mg/L and 303 K temperature was observed as 98.07%. The maximum corrosion inhibition efficiency offered by the investigated S, N-CDs inhibitor is higher as compared to the most of the carbon dots used as corrosion inhibitors and reported in literature [23–32]. On the basis of literature survey and best of our knowledge, the corrosion inhibition study of investigated S, N-CDs inhibitor has not been reported. Therefore the present investigation is novel and value addition in the area of design and development of effective water soluble eco-friendly corrosion inhibitors. From Table 1, the values of ƞ% decreases with increase in temperature because at higher temperature desorption of adsorbed inhibitor molecules on MS surface occurs which leads to decrease in corrosion inhibition efficiency [44]. The increase in temperature may also increase the S, N-CDs mobility, which is also responsible for lower inhibition efficiency.

The adsorption of N, S-CDs on MS surface occurs via donation of lone pair electrons of nitrogen and sulfur to the vacant d orbital of iron present in mild steel. The higher ƞ% of S, N-CDs is due to the presence of abundant nitrogen and sulfur containing functional group as validated by the FTIR, which supports the adsorption of studied inhibitor on MS facet.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of exposure time on corrosion inhibition performance of inhibitors
ce:section.ce:para: The corrosion inhibition performance of N, S-CDs was carried out at several set of time (6–48 h) at 200 mg/ L of concentration and 303 K temperature, the acquired data is represented in Table 2 and Fig. 6. The rate of corrosion was noticed to enhance on enhancing the time of exposure. The corrosion inhibition efficiency after 6 h of immersion time for N, S-CDs was 98.07% and after 48 h it was reduced to 91.58%. This reduction in efficiency is due to the steady desorption of N, S-CDs, adsorbed protective layers on MS surface in the aggressive solution at higher exposure time.

Calculations of activation and thermodynamic parameters play a vital role to know the inhibition mechanism. These parameters were calculated from the weight loss measurement concentration varying from 15 to 200 mg/L concentration at various temperatures. The apparent activation energy (E a) for MS dissolution in aggressive solution was determined by the following equation:
(5)
log
C
R
=
− Ea
2.303 RT
+ log
P

Plot of log C R vs 1000/T is shown in Fig. 7(a) and Ea was calculated from the slope of the straight lines. The obtained values of E a are shown in Table 3. The enthalpy of activation (ΔH*) and entropy of activation (ΔS*) values were determined by using transition state equation:
(6)
C
R
=
RT
NH
e
Δ S •
R
e
− Δ H •
RT

Plot of log (C
R / T) vs 1000 /T is shown in Fig. 7(b) and the values of ΔH* and ΔS* were estimated by the slope and intercept of the straight lines, respectively and obtained data is represented in Table 3. It is apparent from the Table 3 that E a values in the presence of N, S-CDs were higher than that of the blank, suggesting that the metal dissolution activation energy enhances by the formation of higher energy barrier by the adsorbed inhibitor molecules at the mild steel surface. It suggests that in the presence of S, N-CDs corrosion process slows down. The positive values of ΔH* for blank and every concentration of studied inhibitor reveals that presence of inhibitor does not affected the process of corrosion. The ΔS* values were detected to be negative for N, S-CDs. This finding reflects the association of molecules of inhibitor on MS surface during rate determining step [45]. The increase in ΔS* values with increase in inhibitor concentration suggested removal of large number of adsorbed water molecules from the mild steel surface during the adsorption of one molecules of N, S-CDs on mild steel surface.

To get the information about the mechanism of interaction between MS surface and molecules of inhibitor, it is vital to find out the suitable adsorption isotherm. Several adsorption isotherms (Frundlich, Frumkin, Temkin, Langmuir) were tested. Out of these Langmuir adsorption isotherm was found to be best fit so the adsorption of N, S-CDs was explained by this model. Langmuir adsorption isotherm is denoted by the equation:
(7)
C
i n h
θ
=
1
K
ads
+
C
inh

Plot of C inh and C/θ are displayed in Fig. 8. The obtained value of slope and correlation coefficient (R 2) for N, S-CDs are noted in Table 4. It is visible from the Table 4 that the value of slope and R2 are near to unity, validating adsorption of N, S-CDs on MS follow Langmuir adsorption isotherm. Langmuir adsorption isotherm comprises mono layer adsorption, so studied inhibitor molecules follow mono layer adsorption and therefore retards the further MS samples dissolution but on increasing temperature, these adsorbed thin film results in desorption from the MS surface which leads to higher rate of corrosion. The value of adsorption equilibrium constant (K ads) was evaluated from the intercept of Langmuir adsorption isotherm and noted in Table 4. The free energy of adsorption
( Δ G
ads
0
)
, was evaluated by the following equation and evaluated values are listed in Table 4.
(8)
Δ G
ads
0
= − RT ln
55.5
K
ads

Δ G
ads
0
values give information about the nature of adsorption of inhibitor. The negative value of
Δ G
ads
0
indicate the spontaneous adsorption of molecules of N, S-CDs on MS surface. Adsorption type can be found from
Δ G
ads
0
values. When
Δ G
ads
0
values are more negative than −40 kJ mol−1, it suggested chemisorption, and if
Δ G
ads
0
are around and less negative than –20 kJ mol−1, the adsorption type is physisorption. In this study,
Δ G
ads
0
values are −37.25 to –40.36, indicated mixed type adsorption inhibitor molecules on mild steel surface. [46].

Potentiodynamic polarization is an electrochemical method to evaluate corrosion rate and corrosion inhibition efficiency offered by a corrosion inhibitor for a particular metal in a particular corrosive environment. Potentiodynamic polarization consist of cathodic and anodic partial reactions. The Potentiodynamic polarization curves of MS electrode without and with several concentration of synthesized N, S-CDs inhibitor in 15% HCl solution at 303 K is displayed in Fig. 9. The corrosion parameters such as corrosion potential (E Corr), corrosion current density (i corr), anodic Tafel slope (βa) and cathodic Tafel slope (βc) determined from the polarization curves are given in Table 5. The nature of polarization curves remains same in absence and presence of inhibitor suggested that mechanism of MS corrosion does not alter after addition of inhibitor in 15% HCl solution. Both anodic and cathodic curves shifted towards lower values of corrosion current density, reports the mixed (anodic as well as cathodic) nature of N, S- CDs inhibitor. The values of βa and βc as shown in Table 5 are almost same in absence and presence of N, S- CDs, also supports that mechanism of corrosion does not affected by addition of inhibitor. It is visible from Table 5 that i corr values decreases on increasing the concentration of N, S- CDs inhibitor, suggested the adsorption of N, S- CDs on MS surface. The change in E Corr values after addition of inhibitor in HCl medium is lower than ± 85 mV as compare to the blank indicating that N, S- CDs comes in the category of mixed type inhibitor [47]. In presence of N, S- CDs, measured E corr values shifted slightly towards positive direction as compare to the blank suggesting that N, S- CDs act as a mix type inhibitor with predominantly anodic nature. The minor change in the βa and βc values in presence of N, S- CDs suggested that N, S- CDs controlled both anode and cathode reaction. The
ƞ %
values of N, S-CDs was found as 75.0% at 15 mg/ L and 94.7% at 200 mg/ L concentration, respectively. Thus, it can be inferred that protective N, S- CDs adsorption film is formed on MS surface, thereby making a shield against the acidic attack to give inhibitive ability for MS corrosion in 15% HCl solution.

Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS) was used to study the behavior of the metal/solution interface in the absence and presence of studied inhibitor. The Nyquist and Bode plots for MS in HCl solution at 303 K, with and without N, S-CDs of different concentrations are shown in Fig. 7(a). These plots were achieved after 30 min immersion of MS samples with and without N, S-CDs inhibitor at 303 K temperature. The EIS data obtained from Nyquist plots are shown in Table 6. The Nyquist plots of N, S-CDs show only one-time constant corresponding to one capacitive loop, recommended that the kinetics of the electrochemical system is limited by a charge-transfer control process. It can be seen from Fig. 10 (a) that the shape of the Nquist plot with and without inhibitor is almost same but diameter of the semicircle increases on addition of inhibitor, reveals that addition of N, S-CDs improves the impedance but makes no modification in other electrochemical properties. Imperfection in the semicircle with one-time constant is because of the surface roughness of the MS electrode [48]. The diameter of capacitive loop increases with increase in concentration of added N, S-CDs inhibitor in 15% HCl solution. This regular increase in diameter demonstrates that the adsorption of N, S-CDs molecules takes place on the surface of MS or formation of thin film of N, S-CDs on MS surface [49]. This protective film of inhibitor reduces the active surface area of the MS and increases its corrosion resistance behavior in15% HCl solution. The equivalent circuit, showed in Fig. 10 (b) was utilized for fitting the Nyquist plots and to get the experimental data such as solution resistance (R s), polarization resistance (R p) and the constant phase element (CPE). The CPE was used in equivalent circuit instead of double-layer capacitance (C dl) for good fitting of experimental data. The values of C dl, and ƞ% were determined by the equations
(9)
C
dl
= (
Y
0
R
P
1 − n
)
1
n
(10)
η % =
R
P
inh
−
R
P
0
R
P
inh
× 100

It is evident from Fig. 10(a) that there is a good correlation between the theoretical data (dotted lines) and experimental data (hard liner), demonstrating that the equivalent circuit as shown in Fig. 10(b) is appropriate for fitting the Nyquist plots. The EIS parameter are displayed in Table 6. From EIS data, it can be seen that R p values increases while C dl values decreases with increase in concentration of N, S-CDs inhibitor Thereby, it can be stated that N, S-CDs molecules adsorbed on MS surface by replacement of water molecules on MS surface, leading to the decrease in rate of MS dissolution [50]. As a result, increase in ƞ% values occur with increase in concentration of N, S-CDs inhibitor and reaching 96.3% at 200 mg / L concentration and at 303 K temperature. This result confirms the adsorption of inhibitors on MS surface.

The Bode plots are represented in Fig. 11. The single peak was seen in Bode plot (Fig. 11b) for N, S-CDs revealed the presence of single time constant as also presented in Nyquist plots. It is visible from the Fig. 11(a) that the magnitude of impedance increases over whole frequency range with increase in concentration of N, S-CDs inhibitor, suggesting the adsorption of inhibitor molecules on MS surface. The frequency range with the maximum phase angle increases as the concentration of N, S-CDs inhibitor increases as shown in Fig. 11(b). This result also suggested the adsorption of N, S-CDs inhibitor molecules on MS surface.

Contact angle measurement was performed to validate the formation of protective layer of N, S-CDs inhibitor molecules on MS surface. This measurement was carried out by placing 4 μl droplet of distilled water on MS surface. Fig. 12 displayed the images of contact angle measurement of polished sample, sample submerged in 15% HCl solution and sample submerged with inhibitor. The contact angle of polished sample (Fig. 12a) was seen at 67.5° which was reduced to 36.2° when immersed in acid solution (Fig. 12b). The contact angle of sample which was immersed with inhibitor (Fig. 12c) was increased to 75.0°. Reduction in wet ability and enhancement in hydrophobic contact angle indicated the adsorption of synthesized N, S-CDs inhibitor molecules on MS surface.

To get the information about the topographical details of MS sample, FESEM analysis was accomplished. Fig. 13 shows the SEM images of polished MS sample as well as MS sample submersed in aggressive solution without and with 200 mg/L of concentration of S, N-CDs inhibitor. Fig. 13(a) shows that the MS sample before submerging in acidic solution is smooth with some abrasive marks. Fig. 13(b) shows that the MS sample after submerging in 15% HCl solution in absence of S, N-CDs, MS surface is badly corroded with so many cracks and pits. Moreover, MS surface in presence of S, N-CDs (Fig. 13c) was improved with less number of cracks and pits as compare to the blank. Hence these results validate the adsorption of molecules of S, N-CDs inhibitor on MS surface.

AFM analysis was performed to know the average roughness of uninhibited and inhibited MS samples. Fig. 14 represents the AFM 3D morphology images of polished sample, blank sample without inhibitor and with S, N-CDs inhibitor along with their respective height profiles. Fig. 14(a) and (d) displayed the AFM image of polished MS sample with average roughness of 74.6 nm and its height profile, respectively.

Fig. 14(b) and (e) displayed the AFM image of blank MS sample with average roughness of 1.9 µm and its height profile, respectively. Fig. 14(c) and (f) displayed the AFM image of sample with N, S-CDs with average roughness of 119.3 nm and its height profile, respectively. There is huge difference in the average roughness of MS surface with N, S-CDs as compare to blank sample (without inhibitor), so it validates the adsorption of N, S-CDs on MS surface and mitigation of the corrosion at a great extent. The height profile of all the samples also justify their roughness values.

To know the chemical nature and adsorption type of protective film formed on the MS surface, XPS analysis of the inhibited MS surface was carried out. Fig. 15 shows the full surface survey scan spectra of MS sample inhibited by N, S-CDs inhibitor. The presence of peaks corresponding to all the elements present in N, S-CDs inhibitor suggested the adsorption of N, S-CDs inhibitor on MS surface.

The high resolution C1s XPS spectra ( Fig. 16 a) could be deconvulated into five peaks with binding energy 283.2, 284.0, 284.7, 285.7 and 287.8 eV assigned to C–C [51], CC [38], CN [39], CO/CS [18,41] and C–S [24], respectively. Fig. 16 (b) shows the characteristics peaks of nitrogen of -CN at 398.3 [37], -C-NH at 399.2 [18], N–Fe [52] at400.6 and =N+H- at 401.7 eV [52]. For the O1s spectra (Fig. 16 c), oxygen signals at 530.6, 531.2, and 532.2 eV are associated to the Fe2O3 [53] ,CO [6] and C–OH [6], respectively. The S2p spectra (Fig. 16 d), exhibits four peaks at 161.2, 163.6, 168.0 and 169.3 eV allotted to CS, C–S, S–Fe and H2O, respectively [43]. The Fe2p spectra (Fig. 16e), shows doublet for Fe2p3/2 and Fe2p1/2 at t 710.5 eV and 722.4 eV, respectively. The Fe2p3/2 spectra exhibits four peaks, peak at lower binding energy (708.3 eV) ascribed to metallic iron, peak at 709.3 eV allotted to Fe2+ such as Fe3O4, peak at 710.5 allotted to Fe3+ such as Fe2O3 and FeOOH and peak at 714.0 eV was attributed to the satellite of Fe (Ⅲ) [53,54].

The N, S-CDs adsorbed on MS surface through nitrogen and sulfur by donor- acceptor bonds and influence of charge on N, S-CDs also has a significant role in adsorption through electrostatic force of attraction. As the prepared carbon dots contain numerous functional groups, including hydroxyl, amino and carbonyl groups so it is strongly adsorbed on MS steel surface and prevents its corrosion. The presence of S-Fe and N-Fe in XPS also demonstrated that functional groups are distributed on the surface of N, S-CDs, because of that they can be strongly bind on MS surface. The potentiodynamic polarization curve reveals that N, S-CDs act as ixed inhibitor, and prevent the metal dissolution process at anode and hydrogen evolution process at cathode. Additionally, carbon dots block the active sites on MS surface and inhibit the corrosion of MS. The presence of functional groups on their surface may result in strong tendency for carbon dots to attract one another and produce enormous agglomerates (nanoparticles) when the residual N, S-CDs are in close proximity. Thus, the carbon dots nanoparticles adhered to the MS surface and formed the protective film which prevents the corrossive medium from the direct contact with mild steel. From contact angle measurement and EIS measurement, contact angle increased from 36.20 to 75.00 and R P increased on addition of synthesized inhibitor in 15% HCl solution, revealed the adsorption and hydrophobicity of the inhibitor. These all properties support that the adsorption of N,S-CDs on MS surface is responsible for the corrosion inhibition of mild steel in 15% HCl solution.

Novel eco-friendly, water-soluble and efficient N, S-CDs corrosion inhibitor for MS in 15% HCl solution was synthesized by hydrothermal method and characterize by FTIR, UV–vis, photoluminescence, XPS and TEM analysis. The corrosion inhibition efficiency offered by the investigated N, S-CDs inhibitor at 200 mg/L of concentration was 98.64 at 303 K temperature which is better than most of the reported similar type of corrosion inhibitors. The corrosion inhibition efficiency of studied N, S-CDs inhibitor increases on increasing concentration and decreases on increasing temperature. Adsorption of N, S-CDs on MS surface followed Langmuir adsorption isotherm. Potentiodynamic measurement suggested the mixed nature of N, S-CDs inhibitor. EIS studies reveals that increase in charge transfer resistance with increase in concentration and decrease in double layer capacitance with increase in concentration, suggesting adsorption of N, S-CDs on MS surface. The SEM, AFM and XPS studies validate the formation of protective film of inhibitor on MS surface.
Vandana Saraswat: Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Software, Validation, Mahendra Yadav: Supervision, Writing – review & editing.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Electrochemical andXPS studies of the corrosion inhibition of carbon steel in hydrochloric acid pickling solutions by 3,5-bis(2-thienylmethyl)-4-amino-1,2,4-triazole
Corros. Sci.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: M. Tourabi, K. Nohair, M. Traisnel, C. Jama, F. Bentiss, Electrochemical andXPS studies of the corrosion inhibition of carbon steel in hydrochloric acid pickling solutions by 3,5-bis(2-thienylmethyl)-4-amino-1,2,4-triazole, Corros. Sci., 75 (2013),123‐133.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Novel quinoline derivatives as green corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in acidic medium: electrochemical, SEM, AFM, and XPS studies
J. Mol. Liq.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Singh, V. Srivastava, M. A. Quraishi, Novel quinoline derivatives as green corrosion inhibitors for mild steel in acidic medium: Electrochemical, SEM, AFM, and XPS studies J. Mol. Liq. 2016, 216, 164‐173.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: 9-Substitutedacridines as effective corrosion inhibitors for mild steel: electrochemical, surface morphology, and computational studies
New J. Chem.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: W. Zhang, Y. Liu, Y. Zhang, L. J. Wang, Y. C. Wu, H. J. Li, 9-Substitutedacridines as effective corrosion inhibitors for mild steel: electrochemical, surface morphology, and computational studies, New J. Chem., 44 (2020), 6464‐6474.
